title: 🔁 Practice Plus: relative clauses & comparatives review

questions.prompt: Complete: "The man ___ lives next door is a doctor."
questions.explanation: "who" is the relative pronoun for people acting as the subject: the man who lives next door. "which" is for things; "whose" shows possession; "where" refers to places.

questions.prompt: Complete: "That's the girl ___ bag was stolen."
questions.explanation: "whose" shows possession (the girl's bag): the girl whose bag was stolen. "who"/"that" replace the person but cannot show possession; "which" is for things.

questions.prompt: Complete: "This is the restaurant ___ we had dinner last night."
questions.explanation: "where" refers to a place and means "in which": the restaurant where we had dinner. "which" would need a preposition (in which); "who" is for people; "whose" shows possession.

questions.prompt: Complete: "This book is ___ than the last one I read."
questions.explanation: Long adjectives form the comparative with "more": more interesting. "interestinger" wrongly adds -er to a long adjective; "most interesting" is the superlative; "more interestinger" double-marks the comparative.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Everest is ___ mountain in the world."
questions.explanation: Comparing one thing with all others needs the superlative with "the": the highest mountain. "the higher" is a comparative (two things); "highest" needs "the"; "the most high" wrongly uses "most" with a short adjective that takes -est.

questions.prompt: Complete: "My new phone is not ___ my old one; they cost the same."
questions.explanation: To say two things are equal (or, with "not", to compare them) we use "as + adjective + as": not as expensive as. "more expensive than" states a difference, which contradicts "they cost the same"; (c) and (d) mix "as/so" with "than", which is incorrect.
